turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion of the Court in case 23-7809, Gutierrez versus Saenz.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: For nearly 15 years, petitioner Ruben Gutierrez has sought DNA testing of evidence recovered from the scene of a murder he was convicted of committing. He urges that DNA evidence together with newly discovered evidence will help him prove that he neither participated nor anticipated the stabbing at issue here. With that, Gutierrez says he can show that he is innocent or at the very least that he should have never been sentenced to death under Texas law. When the local prosecutor refused to test the relevant DNA evidence he kept in his custody, Gutierrez sued the prosecutor under section 1983. He argued that Texas' procedures for obtaining DNA testing violated his rights under the due process clause. The district court agreed, at least in part, and granted a declaratory judgment to that effect. The District Attorney appealed that decision to the Fifth Circuit, but that Court never addressed the merits of Gutierrez's due process claim. Instead, it said that Gutierrez lacked standing to sue the DA in the first place. Even if a federal court declared Texas' procedures unconstitutional, the Court said, the local prosecutor would be unlikely to turn over the physical evidence for DNA testing, so his injury could not be redressed by a favorable court decision. This Court now reverses. We held just two years ago that another Texas prisoner, Rodney Reed, had standing to sue a local prosecutor who denied him access to DNA testing. We explained there that if a federal court concludes that Texas' DNA testing procedures violate due process that court order would eliminate the state prosecutor's reliance on Article 64 as a reason for denying DNA testing. That would remove a barrier between the prisoner and DNA testing and therefore redress his injury, at least in part. The same is true here. The declaratory judgment Gutierrez requested would redress his injury by eliminating the state prosecutor's allegedly unlawful justification for denying DNA testing. Sure, the state prosecutor might eventually find another reason to deny Gutierrez's request for DNA testing. But that does not mean Gutierrez lacks standing to argue that the presently cited reasons violate his rights under the due process clause. Because the Federal Circuit held otherwise, the Court reverses and remands for further proceedings consistent with the opinion. Justice Barrett filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment. Justice Thomas filed a dissenting opinion. Justice Alito filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Thomas and Gorsuch joined.
